title: The 2026 Agentic Era with Gemini Agent Platform: Surviving Cascading Failures and Runaway Cloud Bills.
summary: Google's Gemini Enterprise Agent Platform enables developers to build and govern autonomous agents at scale. The article examines critical challenges: cascading failures when tightly-coupled multi-agent systems crash, token limit overflows that halt workflows, and runaway cloud bills when dependent agents continue consuming API calls. Google offers mitigation via Event Compaction, Agent Observability, and Cloud Assist.
content:
This is a submission for the Google Cloud NEXT Writing Challenge
The shift from simple AI pilots to fully autonomous digital task forces is officially here!
With the release of the Gemini Enterprise Agent Platform at Google Cloud Next '26, developers can now build, scale, and govern agents with the same rigor applied to mission-critical systems.
We are moving beyond standalone models and stepping into a brave new world where agents seamlessly discover and collaborate with each other using the Agent Registry and the universal A2A protocol.
But let us talk about the elephant in the room: What exactly happens when a tightly coupled multi-agent system goes off the rails?
In a distributed agent architecture, you might have a Planner Agent generating data, an Evaluator Agent judging it, and a Simulator Agent running the scenarios. These agents rely heavily on one another. Didn't someone say:
introducing LLMs means our systems will start breaking in entirely new and unexpected ways!
Imagine a central agent accumulating massive amounts of uncompressed context during a complex simulation. Suddenly, it crashes because it exceeded the 1-million context token limit.
The immediate result is a severe cascading failure. Dependent agents are left waiting, causing unusually high latency and complete workflow stalls. The domino effect brings the whole interconnected application to a grinding halt.
Or perhaps something far worse can happen: if some of those dependent agents were constructed slightly wrongly, instead of just waiting for the crashed agent, they may even continue the Agentic loop and keep consuming LLM and API calls, causing a totally unexpected platform bill before you can check your Budget exceeded emails!
Right now, Google Cloud offers features like Event Compaction to force agents to summarize workflows and avoid hitting those massive token limits. We also have Agent Observability to trace underlying reasoning loops and Gemini Cloud Assist to autonomously investigate

zh.title: 2026年代理时代与Gemini Agent平台：应对级联故障和失控的云账单
zh.summary: Google的Gemini企业代理平台使开发者能够大规模构建和管理自主代理。本文探讨了关键挑战：紧耦合多代理系统崩溃导致的级联故障、导致工作流停止的令牌限制溢出，以及依赖代理继续消耗API调用导致的失控云账单。Google通过事件压缩、代理可观测性和Cloud Assist提供缓解方案。

title: Extreme Ownership
summary: The author reflects on applying "Extreme Ownership" principles when firefighting dominated their workplace culture. Instead of just closing tickets, they investigated root causes and escalated data-backed concerns to leadership about unsustainable incident patterns. They realized true ownership means preventing fires from restarting, not just extinguishing them.
content:
I’ve been reading “Extreme Ownership”, and I want to reflect on some ideas through my own experience.
Starting with the first principle itself.
Extreme Ownership.
Years before I had the term, I think I had already wrestled with it.
I joined a company and by day three I was dealing with production incidents with almost no onboarding. Firefighting was normal.
But what struck me was not the fire itself.
It was that people kept fixing consequences while causes were left standing.
So I started treating my role differently.
Not as “close the ticket in front of you,” but as: where are the bottlenecks, why does this keep breaking, what could be improved so the same incidents stop repeating?
That led me into uncomfortable places.
There was a password management solution defended because it was “free,” while its maintenance risk was quietly pushed into operational work. I argued that economics made no sense.
There was critical automation written in ways I believed were deeply unmaintainable, yet heavily relied upon. I offered to take it over and improve it. I was told no.
Developers were constantly bombarded, context switching all day. I suggested acting as a buffer and improving prioritization. Again, no.
Then I pulled rough numbers from Jira.
For every ticket I closed, several more were coming in.
I remember thinking: this does not end in hard work. This ends in collapse.
That was the moment I escalated.
I built a presentation and showed the data to a larger IT group, including skip-level leadership.
Afterward, my department head asked me for the reference.
Nothing changed.
Later I raised concerns to a C-level leader directly, explaining that too much was breaking and the model was unsustainable.
His answer stayed with me.
He said, that’s what firefighters do — you take an axe and a helmet and go into the fire.
And I remember thinking:
No.
A firefighter also tries to stop the building from burning down again.
That was when my understanding of ownership deepened.
I had been

zh.title: 极限所有权
zh.summary: 作者反思了在消防救火主导其工作场所文化时应用"极限所有权"原则的经历。他们不仅仅是关闭工单，而是调查根本原因，并向领导层上报了数据支持的对不可持续事件模式的关切。他们意识到真正的所有权意味着防止火灾重新开始，而不仅仅是扑灭它们。

title: How We Caught Fraud Before the Payment Cleared
summary: A payment fraud detection system was optimized to complete scoring within the 80-120ms payment authorization window. Key improvements included parallelizing feature store lookups (reducing latency by 60%), pre-aggregating velocity counters via stream processing, and isolating model inference in a sidecar to prevent resource contention.
content:
I want to tell you about the day our fraud system started losing.
• Fraud scoring must complete inside the payment authorisation window (~80–120ms available in practice)
• Sequential feature store lookups were our biggest latency killer — parallelising them cut retrieval time by ~60%
• Velocity counters must be pre-aggregated via a stream processor, not computed at request time
• Model inference should run in an isolated sidecar to avoid resource contention
• Always design your fallback scoring path before you need it in an incident

The constraint nobody puts in the architecture diagram
Here is something that doesn’t get written about enough: fraud detection at checkout isn’t a background job. It isn’t something you can do asynchronously and reconcile later. The fraud decision has to fit inside the payment authorisation window — typically somewhere between 150ms and 300ms end-to-end, depending on your payment provider and your checkout UX tolerance.
This latency constraint is the architectural forcing function that shapes everything else. It’s not a performance nice-to-have. It’s a hard boundary the entire system has to be designed around from day one.
We didn’t fully internalise this early enough. That was our first mistake.
What the pipeline actually looked like
Before I get into what went wrong, let me walk through how the system was structured — because the architecture itself was sound, and understanding it matters.
Where it started breaking
Growth is a pressure test. When we were processing hundreds of thousands of transactions a day, our p95 latency was comfortable. The feature store lookups were fast, the model inference was fast, the whole thing felt snappy.
The latency audit
The first thing we did was stop guessing and start measuring. Properly measuring.
❌ BEFORE — Sequential lookups
✅ AFTER — Parallel lookups
What we actually rebuilt
Once we understood the problem clearly, we made three structural changes that transformed the system’s behaviour under lo

zh.title: 我们如何在支付清算前发现欺诈
zh.summary: 支付欺诈检测系统被优化为在80-120毫秒的支付授权窗口内完成评分。关键改进包括并行化特征存储查找（将延迟降低60%）、通过流处理预聚合速度计数器，以及在sidecar中隔离模型推理以防止资源争用。

title: The 5 AWS charges silently draining your budget (and how to fix each one)
summary: This article identifies five overlooked AWS cost drivers: continuously-running dev/staging RDS instances, expensive on-demand pricing, orphaned EBS volumes, untired S3 storage, and data transfer costs. Each issue includes practical fixes with documented savings ranging from 30% to 65% reduction.
content:
Every AWS bill tells a story. After couple of years as a DevOps engineer reviewing cloud infrastructure, I've learned to read that story, and it almost always has the same five chapters.
1. Dev/Staging RDS Running 24/7
What it costs: A db.r5.xlarge running continuously costs ~$876/month. If it's only used 8 hours a day on weekdays, you're paying for 720 hours and using ~170.
Why it happens: The database got created for a sprint, the sprint ended, and nobody scheduled a shutdown.
The fix — Lambda stop/start schedule (20 minutes to implement):
`# Create an IAM role for Lambda with RDS stop/start permissions
aws events put-rule \
aws events put-rule \
Savings: 65% reduction on affected instances.
2. Everything on On-Demand Pricing
What it costs: On-Demand is the list price the most expensive way to run EC2. If you've had stable workloads running for 6+ months, you're paying a significant premium for flexibility you're not using.
The fix — Compute Savings Plans:
shell



  
  
  Check Savings Plans recommendations


shell


Purchase a 1-year no-upfront Compute Savings Plan for your baseline usage. Zero architecture change. Zero risk.
Savings: 30–45% on covered compute.
3. Unattached EBS Volumes
What it costs: $0.10/GB/month sounds trivial. 50 forgotten 100GB volumes from a migration two years ago = $500/month. Every month. For nothing.
Why it happens: When you terminate an EC2 instance, AWS doesn't automatically delete the attached EBS volume unless you explicitly configured it to do so.
bash# List all unattached EBS volumes with their size and creation date
aws ec2 describe-volumes \


# Delete a specific volume (after verifying you don't need it)


Before deleting: create a snapshot if there's any chance the data is needed. Snapshots cost ~$0.05/GB/month — a fraction of the volume cost.
4. S3 Storage Never Tiered
What it costs: S3 Standard is $0.023/GB/month. S3 Glacier Instant Retrieval is $0.004/GB/month. Data from 2022 that nobody has accessed sitting in Standard c

zh.title: 5个正在暗中消耗你AWS预算的费用（以及如何逐一修复）
zh.summary: 本文识别了五个被忽视的AWS成本驱动因素：持续运行的开发/测试RDS实例、昂贵的按需定价、孤立的EBS卷、分层不足的S3存储和数据传输成本。每个问题都附带实用的修复方案，已验证的节省范围从30%到65%。

title: How 1,000 Wei Can Drain Protocol Fees: A Deep Dive into CoW Protocol Rounding Errors
summary: The CoW Protocol's GPv2Settlement contract contains a vulnerability where integer division rounding allows malicious solvers to execute dust trades (1,000 wei) with zero protocol fees. This enables unfair advantage and unintended protocol revenue loss through exploitation of the mathematical precision limits in Solidity.
content:
In this post, I’ll show how a malicious solver can exploit integer division in GPv2Settlement to execute trades with zero protocol fees.
The Vulnerability: Death by a Thousand Cuts
When a solver executes a partial fill, the protocol calculates the proportional fee using the following formula:
executedFeeAmount= 
Since Solidity doesn't support floating-point numbers, it uses integer division, which always rounds down.
The Attack Vector
Proof of Concept (PoC)
Solidity
// Solver executes a "dust" trade of 1000 wei
uint256 executedAmount = 1000; 

// Proportional fee calculation: (1e18 * 1000) / 100e18
uint256 executedFeeAmount = (feeAmount * executedAmount) / sellAmount;

console.log("Executed Amount (wei):", executedAmount);
console.log("Calculated Fee (wei): ", executedFeeAmount);

assertEq(executedFeeAmount, 0, "Fee should be rounded to zero");

}
As shown in my terminal, the test passed with a zero fee result, confirming the "Fee Leakage" vulnerability.
Impact & Mitigation
Protocol Revenue Loss: The DAO loses its intended cut of the volume.
Unfair Advantage: Solvers can bypass the cost of doing business on the protocol.
Recommended Fix:
Conclusion
You can find the full PoC and my research notes in my GitHub repository:
https://github.com/rdin777/contracts_cowprotocol
About the Author:
I am a Smart Contract Auditor and Security Researcher specializing in vulnerability research (Log Injection, DoS, and Math errors). Currently looking for remote opportunities in Web3 security.


  
  
  solidity #web3 #security #ethereum #foundry

zh.title: 1,000 Wei如何掏空协议费用：CoW协议舍入错误深度分析
zh.summary: CoW协议的GPv2Settlement合约存在一个漏洞，其中整数除法舍入允许恶意求解器执行灰尘交易（1,000 wei）而无需支付协议费用。这通过利用Solidity中的数学精度限制，实现了不公平的优势并导致意外的协议收入损失。

title: I Let An AI Coding Agent Touch My Codebase Here’s What It Broke, Saved, And Secretly Cost Me
summary: After testing an AI coding agent on a real codebase, the author found significant mixed results: valuable automation for repetitive edits and test scaffolding, but unexpected refactors that broke authentication flows and introduced hidden costs. The experience demonstrates that AI agents need guardrails and lack understanding of product context.
content:
I let an AI coding agent loose inside a real codebase, and yeah, it was impressive for about five minutes. 
Then the weird stuff started. 
A clean refactor broke auth, renamed things no one asked for, and quietly added a cost line item nobody mentions in demos. 
But it also saved hours on boring edits, test scaffolding, and repo search. 
That’s the truth most blog posts skip. AI coding agents are useful, but they are not magic, and they are definitely not free. 
If you’re thinking about shipping faster, this is the part you should read first today.
Yes, an AI coding agent can speed up delivery.
No, it should not touch your codebase without guardrails.
That’s the whole story in one breath. GitHub says its Copilot coding agent can research a repository, create an implementation plan, and make code changes on a branch before a pull request is opened. That sounds great, and honestly, sometimes it is. But “can change code” is not the same as “understands your product.”
And that gap is where things got interesting.
First, the good news.
The agent was fast at the work humans avoid. It handled repetitive edits, generated test cases that were decent enough to start from, and found related files quicker than a junior dev on day three. It also helped untangle a few utility functions that had been sitting untouched because nobody wanted to open that mess.
That matters. In a busy team, speed on low-risk cleanup is real value. For product teams evaluating a software development company, this is exactly where AI starts paying off: not in replacing engineers, but in removing drag.
So yes, it saved time. A lot of it, actually.
Now the part people usually hide under screenshots and hype.
The agent made a “safe” refactor across multiple files and quietly changed a naming pattern tied to an internal auth flow. No giant red flag. No dramatic warning. Just one small assumption, made confidently, that broke a working path.
It also over-cleaned code that looked unused but was still tied to

zh.title: 我让一个AI编码代理接触我的代码库 看看它破坏、保存和暗中花费了什么
zh.summary: 在真实代码库上测试AI编码代理后，作者发现了复杂的混合结果：对重复编辑和测试脚手架的宝贵自动化，但意外的重构破坏了身份验证流程并引入了隐藏成本。这次经历表明AI代理需要更好的护栏并缺乏对产品背景的理解。

title: 8 AI Coding Agents That Actually Ship Production Code in 2026
summary: This article reviews eight AI coding agents actively deployed in production, ranked by real-world effectiveness on actual repositories rather than demos. Featured tools include Claude Code, Aider, and Cursor, evaluated on their ability to handle real test suites, git workflows, and production configurations.
content:
Most "AI agent" articles list frameworks for building agents.
This isn't that.
Using an AI coding agent and building one are completely different problems. Using one means file system access, real test suites, actual PRs, production config files. The bar is higher than "it autocompletes well."
I've been watching teams use — and abandon — these tools on real codebases. Not demos. Not toy repos. Here's what's actually moving the needle in 2026.
I'm not ranking by GitHub stars or VC funding. I'm ranking by:
Does it work on codebases you didn't write? Most agents fall apart past 3 files.
Does it respect your existing workflow? Git, CI, tests — not a sandbox.
Can it hold context across multiple files? The whole point.
Does it know when to stop and ask? Autonomy without judgment is a liability.
Would I trust it on a Friday afternoon deploy? Honest test.
TL;DR: AI coding agents are past the demo phase — the ones worth using in 2026 edit real files, run real tests, and ask before they touch your main branch.

Claude Code — Terminal-native agent that reasons before it acts
Aider — Git-aware CLI pair programmer, fully open source
Cursor — AI-first editor with a multi-file agent mode that actually ships
OpenHands — Self-hostable autonomous software engineer
Cline — VS Code agent that reads your repo and deploys
pompelmi — The security check before AI-generated code ships
SWE-agent — Princeton's agent that resolves GitHub issues on its own
Sweep — Turns GitHub issues into PRs without a keystroke
What it is: A CLI agent from Anthropic that runs in your terminal, reads your files, runs shell commands, and edits code across your entire repository without leaving the command line.
Why it matters in 2026: Most AI tools work on files you paste into a chat window. Claude Code works on your actual project — it greps, runs tests, reads git history, and makes decisions based on what it finds. The key differentiator is that it asks before doing anything destructive, which is the behavior

zh.title: 8个在2026年真正交付生产代码的AI编码代理
zh.summary: 本文回顾了八个在生产环境中积极部署的AI编码代理,根据在实际存储库上的真实效果而非演示进行排名。所涵盖的工具包括Claude Code、Aider和Cursor,评估标准是它们处理真实测试套件、Git工作流和生产配置的能力。

title: I Gave AI Agents Real Excel. They Did Not Use It Like I Expected - Proven By 90 Days of Telemetry.
summary: The author built an Excel MCP server connecting AI agents directly to the Excel application rather than just file parsers, enabling real calculation engine and VBA execution. Telemetry from 90 days and 2,908 users revealed unexpected growth patterns and showed how agents behave differently when given access to real Excel functionality.
content:
90 days of telemetry from an open-source MCP server that drives the actual Excel desktop app. The numbers were not where I thought they would be.
Half a year ago I asked a simple question: why can AI agents write a React app from scratch but choke on Revenue Model v27 final final.xlsx?
The answer turned out to be boring and important.
Agents had spreadsheet libraries. They did not have Excel.
So I built Excel MCP Server: an open-source MCP server that drives the real Excel desktop application through COM automation. Not a .xlsx parser. Not Open XML. The actual app, with its calculation engine, VBA, Power Query, pivot caches, and all the quirks that make a workbook a workbook.
Every time I talk about this, someone says "why not use a file-based Excel library?"
For generating a .xlsx from a server, those libraries are great. Linux containers, no Office install, fast.
They are not Excel.
They do not run Excel's calculation engine. They do not execute VBA. They do not refresh Power Query. They do not touch the COM object model. They cannot tell you whether the report looks right.
For generating spreadsheets, that is fine. For automating workbooks that already run a business, it is the difference between "I edited a file Excel can open" and "I worked inside Excel."
Excel MCP Server is for the second case. The workbook is treated as an application, not a zip of XML.
AI assistant
  -> MCP protocol
  -> Excel MCP Server  (in-process MCP, or named-pipe CLI daemon)
  -> Excel COM automation
  -> Real Excel.exe with the workbook open

That last line is the entire thesis. 90 days later I have telemetry. The numbers told me things I did not expect.
90 days, anonymous, opt-in: 2,908 users, 86,090 sessions, 488,548 tool invocations.
Growth was sharper than I expected. Weekly users went from 84 to 1,209. Weekly sessions from 310 to 11,769. February: 800 monthly users. March: 1,682. April was on track to clear 2,000 before the month closed.
Fine. Growth charts are nice. Here is the

zh.title: 我给了AI代理真正的Excel。它们没有按我预期的方式使用它——通过90天的遥测数据验证
zh.summary: 作者构建了一个Excel MCP服务器,将AI代理直接连接到Excel应用程序而不仅仅是文件解析器,支持真实的计算引擎和VBA执行。来自90天和2,908个用户的遥测数据揭示了意外的增长模式,并展示了当代理可以访问真实Excel功能时的不同行为方式。

title: How to Actually Use Claude Design
summary: Claude Design is an Anthropic Labs product that converts prompts into interactive prototypes, presentations, and one-pagers using Claude Opus 4.7, with seamless handoff to Claude Code for development. The guide provides best practices for effective prompting, iteration, and when to use the tool versus alternatives like Figma.
content:
Anthropic just shipped Claude Design, a new Labs product that turns prompts into working designs, interactive prototypes, slide decks, and one-pagers. It's powered by Claude Opus 4.7 and - most interestingly for developers - hands off directly to Claude Code when you're ready to build.
This guide walks you through everything you need to actually use it: what it is, how to set it up, how to prompt it well, how to iterate, how to hand off to engineering, and what to avoid. By the end you'll be able to take a project from idea to shipped prototype without leaving the tool.

Who can use it. Claude Design is in research preview and available on Pro, Max, Team, and Enterprise plans. On Enterprise it's default off, so if you don't see it, ask your admin to provision you.
What it's for. Reach for Claude Design when you need to produce interactive prototypes (working HTML/CSS with real states you can click through), marketing pages like landing pages and pricing sections, internal tools like admin dashboards or review queues, presentations and one-pagers, and app flows like onboarding or multi-step forms.
What it isn't for. Don't reach for it when you need pixel-perfect finishing work on an existing Figma file, or when you're doing brand-identity work like logos, illustrations, or photography. For polish and finishing, export to Canva and continue there.
Set expectations for the output. The first generation is a starting point, never a finished artifact. The real value shows up in the second, third, and fourth rounds of iteration. Plan for that - don't judge the tool by its first response.
Open a project. Go to claude.ai/design and create a new project. The interface splits into two panes: chat on the left, canvas on the right. Everything else builds on that structure.
Feed it context before you prompt. This is the step most people skip, and it's the one that determines whether your output looks generic or looks like your product. Before you write your first prompt, attach w

zh.title: 如何实际使用Claude Design
zh.summary: Claude Design是一个Anthropic Labs产品,使用Claude Opus 4.7将提示转换为交互式原型、演示文稿和单页文档,并可无缝切换到Claude Code进行开发。该指南提供了有效提示、迭代的最佳实践,以及何时使用该工具与Figma等替代方案。

title: The Real Risk of AI Is Not Job Loss
summary: Rather than job loss, the article argues AI's real risk is the hidden obsolescence of workers who become dependent on automation for execution tasks while failing to develop judgment and decision-making skills. Those who thrive will extend their thinking through AI rather than replace it, operating at higher levels of abstraction.
content:
Most conversations around AI still revolve around one fear:
Job loss.
Breaking the Expectation
We tend to look for visible disruption.
People are not losing jobs.
They’re still employed.
The Insight
AI is not just replacing tasks.
average becomes invisible
speed becomes default
output becomes abundant
So value shifts elsewhere.
clarity
judgment
problem selection
decision-making
And many people are not adapting to that shift.
What This Looks Like in Reality
This is how it shows up:
Someone completes tasks faster… but doesn’t think deeper
Someone produces more… but contributes less insight
Someone relies on AI… but loses independent reasoning
Nothing looks broken.
The Deeper Risk
The real risk is not unemployment.
Because when your contribution is:
execution
formatting
surface-level output
AI doesn’t need to replace you completely.
The New Reality
The people who will thrive are not those who:
resist AI
or blindly depend on it
But those who:
use it to extend their thinking
develop sharper judgment
operate at a higher level of abstraction
Because that’s where AI still depends on humans.
The Reflection
Job loss is visible.
The more I work with AI, the clearer this becomes:
The real risk is not that AI takes your job.
It’s that it changes the game… and you don’t realize you’re no longer playing at the highest level.
Further Reading Recommendation:
For Newsletter (business-focused):
Follow my newsletter for practical ideas on using AI to grow businesses, improve decisions, and create leverage. If you want signal over noise in the age of AI, you’ll enjoy reading it: Join Today
For Twitter/X (AI-focused):
Follow me on X where I share original thoughts on AI, emerging technology, and where the future is heading. If you care about intelligent signals beyond the headlines, let’s connect there: Visit Now

zh.title: AI的真正风险不是失业
zh.summary: 与其说是失业，这篇文章主张AI的真正风险在于工人的隐性过时——那些依赖自动化执行任务却未能培养判断和决策能力的工人会变得陈旧。真正蓬勃发展的人会通过AI来扩展思维而非替代思维，在更高的抽象层次上运作。

title: Week 1: Hassles & Tackling Problems
summary: A personal learning journal entry documenting the author's start with The Odin Project, emphasizing the importance of building strong foundations rather than skipping basics. They also discuss a hardware upgrade project to install an NVMe SSD to support their learning environment.
content:
Introduction


Hello, I almost forgot I had to post on the 30th. I probably would have forgotten if I hadn’t checked my calendar last night.
So, I started learning from The Odin Project, and I’ll be honest — the first few chapters felt a bit redundant. But I made a decision before starting: I’m not going to skip anything.
Even if something feels basic, I want to build a strong foundation this time instead of rushing ahead and getting stuck later (which has definitely happened before). 
And I’m glad I didn’t skip.
One word that really stuck with me: grit.
So, As guided by The Odin Project I must install WSL or, Linux on Dual boot or, Do Only Linux installation. And, My PC sucks (1 TB HDD, 128 GB SSD) and, I've finally decided to spend ~70%(The prices 😭) of my savings on this 1 TB NVME (M.2 SSD) to replace. Yeah a hassle, but I'll continue the lessons till the SSD arrives! and hope my SSD doesn't give completely under Low space messages.
P.S.: Next post on 7th May' 26.

zh.title: 第1周：困难与解决问题
zh.summary: 一篇个人学习日记，记录作者开始The Odin Project的经历，强调建立坚实基础而不是跳过基础知识的重要性。作者还讨论了安装NVMe SSD的硬件升级项目，以支持他们的学习环境。

title: 🏗️ Building High-Quality AI Agents 🤖 — A Comprehensive, Actionable Field Guide 📘
summary: A comprehensive guide synthesizing production lessons from agents like Claude Code and OpenHands, covering architecture from mental models to anti-patterns across 16 sections. The core insight: agent quality depends primarily on harness engineering (prompts, tools, memory, observability) rather than underlying model capability.
content:
A synthesis of hard-won lessons from Claude Code, OpenHands, SWE-agent, GoClaw, Nanobot, PicoClaw, and the emerging discipline of harness engineering. This is the guide we wish existed when we started building agents.
The goal: an AI agent that is fast, scalable, capable, reliable, efficient, and secure — not by accident, but by design.
Read top-to-bottom for the full mental model. Each section builds on the previous one.
Skim the boxes if you only want the takeaways — every section ends with an Actionable rules box.
Jump to Part 14 — The Build-Your-Own Roadmap if you already know the theory and want a sequenced plan.
Bookmark Part 15 — Anti-Patterns for design reviews.
⚡ Part 0 — The Core Equation
🧠 Part 1 — Mental Model: What an AI Agent Actually Is
🔄 Part 2 — The Agent Loop (the Kernel)
🛠️ Part 3 — Tools: The Agent's Hands
💭 Part 4 — Context Engineering
💾 Part 5 — Memory (Long-Term Knowledge)
⚡ Part 6 — Concurrency & Multi-Agent Patterns
🔧 Part 7 — Reliability: Error Recovery, Stuck Detection, Autosubmit
🔒 Part 8 — Security: Defense-in-Depth
🏢 Part 9 — Multi-Tenancy from Day One
🚀 Part 10 — Performance & Efficiency
🔌 Part 11 — Provider Abstraction & Resilience
📡 Part 12 — Channels & Integration Surface
📊 Part 13 — Observability & Evaluation
🗺️ Part 14 — The Build-Your-Own Roadmap
⚠️ Part 15 — Anti-Patterns to Avoid
🎯 Part 16 — Closing: The Harness Mindset
The single most important insight in agent engineering, framed by Vivek Trivedy of LangChain: "If you're not the model, you're the harness."
   Reliability  ≈  Model capability  ×  Harness quality
                          (fixed)        (your job)

The model is fixed for the duration of your project. The harness — system prompts, tools, sandboxes, memory, hooks, orchestration, observability — is where 80% of agent quality comes from. After hundreds of production agent sessions across many teams, the pattern is consistent:
It's almost never a model problem. It's a configuration problem.
Teams who b

zh.title: 🏗️ 构建高质量AI代理 🤖 — 综合实用现场指南 📘

title: The 5 Reasons AI Can't Touch Java Developers
summary: While 92% of developers use AI tools and 41% of shipped code is AI-generated, 45% of AI-generated code fails security reviews—with Java failing at a brutal 72% rate. Java powers critical infrastructure at 90% of Fortune 500 companies and 70-90% of major global banks, making it mission-critical code where failures trigger audits and regulatory scrutiny. The combination of high security failure rates and Java's enterprise criticality means AI cannot replace Java developers in the near term.
content:
The numbers everyone is talking about


Let me start by scaring you a little — then I promise I'll calm you down.
According to a recent piece titled "The State of AI-Generated Code in 2026: What the Data Says", 92% of developers now use AI tools, and roughly 41% of all code shipped in 2026 was AI-generated. If you're a developer reading this, those numbers probably feel personal. They should.
But here's the part nobody puts in the headline: 45% of AI-generated code fails its first security review. And the language sitting at the very top of that failure list? Java — at a brutal 72%.
Now hold that thought, because this is exactly where things get interesting.
Java isn't just another language on the list. It's the language quietly running the systems you trust with your money, your identity, and your transactions.
A few stats worth chewing on:
70%–90% of major global banks use Java as a primary or significant part of their stack.
Around 90% of Fortune 500 companies rely on Java in their core systems. (Quick reminder: the Fortune 500 is the list of the 500 largest U.S. corporations by revenue — so we're talking about the backbone of enterprise IT.)
Java holds roughly 30% of the overall software development market.
And it powers about 65% of large-scale enterprise applications.
Translation: when we talk about Java, we're talking about mission-critical infrastructure — the kind of code that, if it breaks, makes the news.
This is the context AI is walking into. And it's exactly why "AI will replace Java developers" is one of those headlines that sounds bold on Twitter but falls apart the moment you've ever shipped code to production at a bank.
I work in core banking and FinTech. I've shipped Java in environments where one bad commit can trigger an audit. From inside that world, here are the five reasons replacing Java developers with AI is, frankly, a fantasy — at least for now.
Enterprises and especially financial institutions are paranoid about security — and they shoul

zh.title: AI无法触及Java开发者的5个原因
zh.summary: 虽然92%的开发者使用AI工具，41%的已发布代码由AI生成，但45%的AI生成代码未能通过安全审查——Java的失败率高达72%。Java为90%的财富500强公司和70-90%的全球主要银行的关键基础设施提供支持，使其成为任务关键型代码，失败会触发审计和监管审查。高安全失败率和Java企业关键性的结合意味着AI在短期内无法取代Java开发者。

title: What Google Cloud NEXT '26 Taught Us About Agent Governance
summary: A Google Cloud NEXT demonstration showed an agent with write access to a financial database being persuaded to make an unauthorized budget change through normal conversation, revealing a critical governance gap. The fix involves implementing agent-specific read-only policies in Agent Gateway to enforce security boundaries, treating agents as unique cryptographic principals with zero-trust access control. This highlights the need for proper governance as AI agents gain access to sensitive systems.
content:
This is a submission for the Google Cloud NEXT Writing Challenge
About halfway through the Developer Keynote, Emma Twersky tried to get the Planner Agent to increase the race budget so every runner could get glow sticks and LED sunglasses.
And to everyone’s surprise, it worked.
The next speaker was invited on stage to explain how to prevent exactly that from happening. 
I couldn’t move on from that because what just happened was one of the most honest and quietly alarming demonstrations I've seen at a tech conference in years. An agent with write access to a financial database was talked into making an unauthorized budget change through normal conversation. 
Not through a hack or an injection attack. Just a user asking nicely.
What she demonstrated, before Ankur Kotwal came on stage to fix it, was a real attack surface. The Planner Agent had access to a Finance MCP server with both read and write tools. Emma asked it, through normal conversation, to increase the budget. The agent complied because nothing told it not to. There was no policy. There was no boundary. There was just a capable agent, a persuasive user, and a write-enabled tool sitting there waiting.
Ankur's fix was genuinely clean. He navigated to Agent Gateway, selected the Planner agent, located the Finance MCP server in its allowed tools, added a condition named read-only-finance, set ReadOnly to True, and saved. The same prompt now fails gracefully.
Agent Identity is more interesting than the UI makes it look. Unlike service accounts, which function like a master keycard shared across an entire system, each deployed agent gets a unique cryptographic identity. Policies attach to that identity. The gateway enforces them on every call. It's zero-trust, properly applied to a new kind of principal: not a user, not a service, but an agent.
This is the architecture the industry needs. The uncomfortable part is that we needed it because agents behave like eager-to-please interns who will do whatever they're a

zh.title: Google Cloud NEXT '26关于智能体治理的启示
zh.summary: Google Cloud NEXT的演示展示了一个拥有金融数据库写入权限的智能体通过正常对话被说服进行未授权的预算变更，暴露了一个关键的治理漏洞。修复方案涉及在Agent Gateway中实施智能体特定的只读策略，将智能体视为具有零信任访问控制的独特加密主体。这突出了当AI智能体获得敏感系统访问权限时实施适当治理的必要性。

title: Sheet Generation API: Structured JSON In, Formatted Spreadsheet Out
summary: The Iteration Layer's Sheet Generation API simplifies spreadsheet creation by accepting a single JSON payload and outputting formatted files in XLSX, CSV, or Markdown formats. Existing spreadsheet libraries require verbose APIs with different interfaces across languages and complex boilerplate for styling and formulas. This unified approach eliminates the need to maintain separate code paths and reduces setup complexity for common spreadsheet tasks.

zh.title: 表格生成API：结构化JSON输入、格式化电子表格输出
zh.summary: Iteration Layer的表格生成API通过接受单个JSON有效载荷并输出XLSX、CSV或Markdown格式的文件来简化电子表格创建。现有电子表格库需要冗长的API、跨语言不同的接口以及复杂的样式和公式样板代码。这种统一方法消除了维护单独代码路径的需要，并降低了常见电子表格任务的设置复杂性。

title: Image Crop API for Smart Cropping and Resizing
summary: The Iteration Layer's Image Transformation API provides cloud-based image resizing and cropping with five different fit strategies without requiring local libraries or server infrastructure. Applications send an image and transformation operations to the API and receive the resized result, eliminating the need to manage Sharp libraries, handle edge cases, or maintain image processing servers.

zh.title: 用于智能裁剪和调整大小的图像裁剪 API
zh.summary: Iteration Layer 的图像转换 API 提供基于云的图像调整大小和裁剪，包含五种不同的适配策略，无需本地库或服务器基础设施。应用程序将图像和转换操作发送到 API 并接收调整大小的结果，消除了管理 Sharp 库、处理边界情况或维护图像处理服务器的需要。

title: Convert Markdown to Styled PDF in n8n
summary: The Iteration Layer's Document Generation API solves a repeated pain point in n8n workflows by converting Markdown directly to professionally styled PDFs with proper headers, footers, page numbers, and typography control. Existing workarounds require multiple nodes, complex JavaScript, or temporary Google Docs, whereas this API accepts Markdown in a JSON definition and renders polished PDFs without additional setup.
content:
The n8n Forum's Most Repeated Question


Search the n8n community forum for "Markdown to PDF" and you will find the same question asked repeatedly, with no satisfying answer. The replies fall into three categories.
"Use a Function node with Puppeteer." This requires a Chromium binary in your n8n environment, headless browser rendering, and a pile of JavaScript to set up page sizes, margins, and print styles. Self-hosted n8n can make this work with enough effort. n8n Cloud cannot — there is no browser binary available.
"Use the HTML-to-PDF community node." These nodes convert raw HTML, not Markdown. You need to convert Markdown to HTML first (another Function node), then style it with inline CSS (another Function node), then pass it to the PDF node. Three nodes of glue code, and the output still looks like a web page printed to PDF — no proper headers, footers, or page numbers.
"Export from Google Docs." Create a Google Doc, write content via the API, export as PDF. This technically works but is not automatable for dynamic content. The Google Docs API for formatting is verbose and fragile, and you are creating throwaway documents just to get a PDF.
None of these produce a professional document with consistent typography, a header on every page, a footer with page numbers, and controlled margins.
Iteration Layer Document Generation accepts Markdown content in paragraph blocks — via the markdown field — and renders it into a fully styled PDF. You control the fonts, page size, margins, header, footer, page numbers, heading styles, link colors, and table styling through a JSON document definition.
The Markdown content goes into "type": "paragraph" blocks with a "markdown" field instead of a "text" field. The rendering engine parses the Markdown and applies your style definitions — headings get your heading font and size, links get your link color, code blocks get your code font, tables get your table styling. The output is a PDF that looks like it came from a design tool

zh.title: 在 n8n 中将 Markdown 转换为样式化 PDF
zh.summary: Iteration Layer 的文档生成 API 通过将 Markdown 直接转换为带有适当页眉、页脚、页码和排版控制的专业样式 PDF，解决了 n8n 工作流中反复出现的痛点。现有的解决方法需要多个节点、复杂的 JavaScript 或临时 Google Docs，而此 API 接受 JSON 定义中的 Markdown 并呈现精美的 PDF，无需额外设置。

title: Process Images From Your Coding Session — Smart Crop, Convert, Compress via MCP
summary: The Iteration Layer's Image Transformation API is available as an MCP server that integrates directly with Claude Code, allowing developers to resize, crop, convert, compress, and chain image operations from chat prompts without leaving their editor. By describing what they need in natural language, developers eliminate the context-switching cost of switching to image processing tools.
content:
Image Processing Shouldn't Break Your Flow


Image processing tasks are small but they interrupt everything. A designer sends over a photo that's the wrong dimensions. A client's logo is a 4MB PNG that needs to be a 40KB WebP. A product photo needs smart-cropping for a thumbnail but the subject is off-center. None of these take more than ten seconds to describe — but each one takes you out of your editor, into a terminal, into an image app, or into a back-and-forth with the designer.
With MCP, your AI assistant handles it directly. The Iteration Layer's Image Transformation API ships as an MCP server — connect it once, and Claude Code or Cursor can resize, smart crop, convert, compress, and chain up to 30 operations together from a chat prompt. You describe what you need, the assistant builds the operation sequence, and you get the result without leaving your session.
Model Context Protocol is an open standard that lets AI assistants discover and call external tools. Think of it as a plugin system for AI — you connect a service, and the assistant can use it when relevant.
When you connect the Image Transformation API as an MCP server, your assistant gains the ability to:
Resize images to specific dimensions
Crop images (including AI-powered smart crop)
Convert between formats (PNG, JPEG, WebP, AVIF)
Chain up to 30 operations in a single request
Apply filters, adjust colors, and optimize file sizes
All from a natural language prompt.
Claude Code supports MCP servers natively. Add the Iteration Layer server with a single command:
claude mcp add iterationlayer --transport http https://api.iterationlayer.com/mcp

The first time you use an Iteration Layer tool, a browser window opens for OAuth authentication. Log in, authorize access, and you're connected. No API keys to manage.
To verify the server is available, start a conversation and ask Claude Code what MCP tools it has access to. You should see transform_image listed among the available tools.
Add to your .cursor/m

zh.title: 从您的编码会话中处理图像 — 通过 MCP 智能裁剪、转换、压缩
zh.summary: Iteration Layer 的图像转换 API 作为 MCP 服务器提供，可直接与 Claude Code 集成，允许开发人员从聊天提示中调整大小、裁剪、转换、压缩和链接图像操作，无需离开编辑器。通过用自然语言描述他们的需求，开发人员消除了切换到图像处理工具的上下文切换成本。

title: Optimize Product Images for Amazon, Shopify, and the Web
summary: Product image optimization is critical for e-commerce and web performance—the median webpage transfers over 2MB of images and optimization directly impacts Core Web Vitals and search rankings. This guide covers format selection (JPEG, WebP, AVIF), compression strategies, responsive delivery, and automation workflows to meet marketplace-specific requirements while improving load times and conversion rates.
content:
Product Images Need to Work for Marketplaces and the Web


If you sell on Amazon or Shopify, image optimization is not just a performance task, it is also a requirements task. Amazon cares about white backgrounds, square dimensions, and JPEG output. Shopify cares about storefront clarity, fast load times, and consistent product presentation. Then your own site still needs responsive image delivery, modern formats, and smaller files for Core Web Vitals.
This guide covers the broad image optimization workflow behind all of those requirements: format selection, compression, responsive delivery, and automation. If you want the marketplace-specific implementations, start with Optimize Product Image for Amazon or Optimize Product Image for Shopify, then come back here for the deeper optimization framework.
The median web page transfers over 2 MB of images. For media-heavy sites — e-commerce, real estate, travel, news — that number climbs to 5-10 MB. On a 4G mobile connection, that's 3-8 seconds of loading just for images.
Google measures this. Largest Contentful Paint (LCP) — the time until the largest visible element renders — is a Core Web Vital that directly affects search ranking. For most pages, the largest element is an image. Slow images mean lower rankings, higher bounce rates, and fewer conversions.
Image optimization isn't a nice-to-have. It's the single highest-impact performance improvement for most websites. This guide covers the complete workflow: choosing formats, compressing effectively, sizing for different screens, and building automated pipelines.
Four formats dominate the web. Each has a specific use case.
The workhorse of web images since 1992. Lossy compression with a quality parameter (1-100) that controls the size-quality tradeoff. Universal browser support.
Use for: photographs, complex images with many colors, images where a small quality loss is acceptable.
Quality guidelines: 80-90 for hero images, 70-80 for thumbnails and cards, 60-70 for back

zh.title: 优化亚马逊、Shopify 和网络的产品图片
zh.summary: 产品图片优化对电子商务和网络性能至关重要——中位数网页传输超过 2MB 的图片，优化直接影响 Core Web Vitals 和搜索排名。本指南涵盖格式选择（JPEG、WebP、AVIF）、压缩策略、响应式传输和自动化工作流，以满足市场特定要求，同时改进加载时间和转化率。

title: Compose Branded Graphics from Your AI Assistant — No Design Tools Required
summary: The Iteration Layer's Image Generation API with MCP integration allows developers to describe branded graphics (OG images, banners, social cards) in natural language and have Claude Code compose them pixel-perfect without design tools. Unlike AI image generators that create abstract images from prompts, this API solves the deterministic composition problem needed for marketing and branded content.
content:
The Branded Image Tax


Every developer who ships products pays the branded image tax. OG images for every blog post and landing page. Event banners for every launch. Email headers for every campaign. Social cards for every product update.
None of these are hard to make. But collectively they eat hours — because every one of them means opening Figma, finding the template, updating the text, fixing the alignment, exporting, uploading. Fifteen minutes per image, multiplied by everything you ship.
Before you reach for an AI image generator: tools like DALL-E or Midjourney generate images from abstract prompts, but they don't produce branded graphics with specific fonts, hex colors, and precise layouts. What you actually need for the above use cases isn't generation — it's composition.
The Iteration Layer's Image Generation API uses a layer-based model. You specify a canvas size and stack layers: backgrounds, rectangles, text, photos, overlays. The output is pixel-perfect and deterministic. Same layers, same result, every time.
MCP connects this to your AI assistant. Describe the layout in plain language — "dark background, white title, red accent line, 1200x630" — and the assistant translates that into the layer structure, calls the API, and returns the image. No Figma, no Photoshop, no code.
MCP is an open standard that lets AI assistants discover and call external tools. It's a plugin system — you connect a service, and the assistant can use it when relevant.
Claude Code supports MCP servers natively. Add the Iteration Layer server with a single command:
claude mcp add iterationlayer --transport http https://api.iterationlayer.com/mcp

The first time you use an Iteration Layer tool, a browser window opens for OAuth authentication. Log in, authorize access, and you're connected. No API keys to manage.
To verify the server is available, start a conversation and ask Claude Code what MCP tools it has access to. You should see generate_image listed among the available too

zh.title: 从您的 AI 助手合成品牌图形 — 无需设计工具
zh.summary: Iteration Layer 的图像生成 API 与 MCP 集成允许开发者用自然语言描述品牌图形（OG 图像、横幅、社交卡片），并让 Claude Code 以像素完美的方式合成它们，无需设计工具。与从提示创建抽象图像的 AI 图像生成器不同，这个 API 解决了营销和品牌内容所需的确定性合成问题。

title: Extract IBANs, Currencies, and Addresses from Financial Documents — Validated, Not Just Strings
summary: The Iteration Layer's Document Extraction API provides purpose-built field types for financial data that validate and normalize results rather than relying on regex patterns. The API validates IBANs against country-specific rules, parses currency amounts according to locale, and structures addresses properly—eliminating false matches that regex patterns cannot distinguish.

zh.title: 从财务文件中提取 IBAN、货币和地址 — 验证，而不仅仅是字符串
zh.summary: Iteration Layer 的文档提取 API 为财务数据提供专门构建的字段类型，可以验证和规范化结果，而不是依赖正则表达式。该 API 根据国家特定的规则验证 IBAN，根据区域设置解析货币金额，并正确构造地址——消除了正则表达式无法区分的假匹配。

title: Generate Hundreds of Social Media Images from a Single Template
summary: The Iteration Layer's Image Generation API enables templating for social media images by defining a layout once and injecting dynamic content per post across different platforms with their specific dimension requirements. This solves the manual repetition problem of creating dozens of similar images monthly with only title changes.

zh.title: 从单个模板生成数百张社交媒体图像
zh.summary: Iteration Layer的图像生成API通过定义一次布局并针对不同平台的特定尺寸要求注入动态内容来实现社交媒体图像的模板化。这解决了每月手动创建数十张仅标题不同的类似图像的重复问题。

title: Generate Formatted Excel Spreadsheets in n8n
summary: The Iteration Layer's Sheet Generation API enables n8n workflows to generate standalone formatted Excel files with headers, formulas, and styling without verbose JavaScript or complex workarounds involving temporary Google Sheets. The declarative JSON definition specifies columns, rows, formatting, and formulas, producing standard XLSX files compatible with any spreadsheet application.
content:
n8n Can Read Spreadsheets but Can't Generate Them


n8n has solid spreadsheet reading support. The Spreadsheet File node parses XLSX and CSV. The Google Sheets node reads and writes rows. But generating a standalone XLSX file — with formatted headers, currency columns, multiple sheets, and formulas — does not have a native answer.
The workarounds are all painful. You can use a Function node with ExcelJS, but you are writing JavaScript to build a spreadsheet cell by cell. You lose the visual workflow benefit of n8n entirely. You can export from Google Sheets, but that means creating a temporary sheet, writing data, formatting it through the Google Sheets API (which has a separate formatting endpoint from the data endpoint), exporting to XLSX, and cleaning up the temporary file. That is five nodes for one spreadsheet.
Some teams use a Puppeteer node to render an HTML table and "print" it to XLSX. This works about as well as you would expect — the formatting is approximate, formulas are impossible, and multiple sheets are out of the question.
Iteration Layer Sheet Generation takes a JSON definition and returns a binary XLSX file. You define sheets, columns, rows, cell formatting, and formulas. The API handles rendering. No code, no temporary files, no export chain.
The definition is declarative. You describe what the spreadsheet should look like — column widths, header styles, cell formats, formulas — and the API builds it. Currency formatting, percentage formatting, date formatting, bold headers, merged cells, and Excel formulas all work through the JSON definition.
The output is a standard XLSX file that opens in Excel, Google Sheets, LibreOffice, or any spreadsheet application. Or request CSV or Markdown format if you need a simpler output.
Here is what we are building in n8n: a weekly automated pipeline that queries sales data from a database, generates a formatted Excel report with multiple sheets, and emails it to the team. Four nodes. No Function nodes. No Googl

zh.title: 在n8n中生成格式化的Excel电子表格
zh.summary: Iteration Layer的Sheet生成API使n8n工作流能够生成独立的格式化Excel文件，包含标题、公式和样式，无需冗长的JavaScript或涉及临时Google表格的复杂解决方案。声明式JSON定义指定了列、行、格式和公式，生成与任何电子表格应用程序兼容的标准XLSX文件。

title: Generate Print-Ready Book Covers Programmatically — A Self-Publisher's Guide
summary: The Iteration Layer's Image Generation API generates print-ready book covers programmatically for self-publishing platforms by defining a cover layout template and rendering it in multiple formats with correct dimensions (KDP paperback, Kindle ebook, audiobook thumbnail). This eliminates the need for expensive designers or manual work per title variant when publishing multiple books.
content:
The Cover Problem for Self-Publishers


You've written the book. Now you need a cover. Every format needs its own — Kindle ebook, KDP paperback, hardcover, audiobook thumbnail. Each with different dimensions, different bleed requirements, different text placement. A professional designer charges $500-1,500 per cover. Canva gives you templates that look like every other self-published book on Amazon.
If you're publishing one book, you hire the designer. If you're publishing ten books a year — or running a publishing platform that handles hundreds of authors — manual design doesn't scale.
The Iteration Layer's Image Generation API generates print-ready book covers from a JSON template. Define your cover layout — background, title, author name, decorative elements, optional cover image — and generate it in one API call. Change the title, get a new cover. Change the format, get the right dimensions. No design tool, no export step, no manual work per variant.
Print-on-demand platforms have strict dimension requirements. Amazon KDP specifies dimensions in inches at 300 DPI. The API works in pixels. The conversion is straightforward.
For a standard 6"x9" paperback front cover at 300 DPI:
Width: 6 x 300 = 1800 pixels
Height: 9 x 300 = 2700 pixels
Common book cover dimensions:



Format
Inches
Pixels at 300 DPI




KDP Paperback (6"x9")
6 x 9
1800 x 2700


KDP Paperback (5.5"x8.5")
5.5 x 8.5
1650 x 2550


Kindle Ebook
—
1600 x 2560


Audiobook Thumbnail
—
3200 x 3200


Square Social Promo
—
1080 x 1080



The same cover template can generate all of these by adjusting the composition dimensions and repositioning the layers.
Here's a complete book cover for a fiction title — dark background, large serif title, author name, decorative line elements:
import { IterationLayer } from "iterationlayer";

const client = new IterationLayer({
  apiKey: "YOUR_API_KEY",
});

const result = await client.generateImage({
  dimensions: {
    width_in_px: 1800,
    height_in_px: 2700,
  },
  o

zh.title: 以编程方式生成印刷就绪的书籍封面——自助出版者指南
zh.summary: Iteration Layer的图像生成API通过定义书籍封面布局模板并以正确的尺寸（KDP平装本、Kindle电子书、有声读物缩略图）多种格式呈现，为自助出版平台以编程方式生成印刷就绪的书籍封面。这消除了在出版多本书籍时为每个标题变体支付昂贵的设计师费用或进行手工工作的需要。
